name: Antarctica
classification_notes: Explicitly described as incorporating Neo-Grotesque principles, with a geometric precision and reduction of strokes typical of that tradition. The family includes a contrast axis alongside weight, width and italic axes.
summary: A neo-grotesque variable font by Ian Party offering 288 static styles and four variable axes including a contrast axis, drawing on the minimalism of Swiss grotesque tradition.
description: Designed by Ian Party at Newglyph, Antarctica is a neo-grotesque sans with a variable font covering weight, width, italic and contrast axes across 288 static styles. The contrast axis sets it apart from straightforward grotesques, allowing movement between a pure sans and a more modulated form. With 547 glyphs per style, it covers an extensive range of Latin-script languages.
foundry_blurb: Newglyph describe Antarctica as a font inspired by the uncharted beauty of Earth's southernmost continent, emphasising simplicity, minimalism and purity. They position it as a variable-first typeface built on Neo-Grotesque principles, with geometric precision and a bold, contemporary reduction of strokes, available in 288 additional single styles alongside the variable font in OTF and WOFF2 formats.

name: Le Grand
classification_notes: Le Grand is a high-contrast display companion to a Romain du Roi interpretation, placing it firmly in the rationalist serif tradition characterised by geometric construction, strong stroke contrast and bracketed serifs with double-serif details on lowercase letters.
summary: A sculptural, high-contrast headline serif by Ian Party, conceived as the display companion to his decades-long reimagining of the Romain du Roi.
description: Where its text sibling Romain du Roi is built for rhythm and readability, Le Grand is theatrical by design, with pronounced stroke contrast and distinctive double serifs on lowercase letters such as 'b', 'd', 'h' and 'l'. The family offers 18 styles alongside variable axes for weight, width and contrast, drawing formal inspiration from crescent shapes and the regal finial of the lowercase 'l'. Ian Party's engagement with the Romain du Roi spans his training at ECAL and The Hague through to the SangBleu and SangBleu Empire families, making this release the fullest expression of that research to date.
foundry_blurb: Newglyph describe Le Grand as a sculptural headline companion to Romain du Roi, together forming a duet of two voices in harmony. Ian Party's lifelong engagement with the Romain du Roi — originally commissioned by Louis XIV in 1692 — is realised here not as a revival but as a synthesis of geometric rationality and elegance, with 108 styles across variable weight, width and contrast axes, plus OpenType alternates and small caps.

name: Romain du Roi
classification_notes: Rooted in the Romain du Roi of 1692, one of the earliest rationalist serifs, Ian Party's interpretation sits firmly in the transitional category with its geometric axis, high contrast, and distinctive double serifs drawn from Enlightenment design principles.
summary: Ian Party's decades-long engagement with the 1692 Romain du Roi has produced a 108-style variable family with axes for weight, width and contrast, split between a text companion and a high-contrast headline face called Le Grand.
description: Commissioned by Louis XIV and designed by the Bignon Commission, the original Romain du Roi was among the first rationalist typefaces; Party's version honours that geometric rigour while pushing it toward something distinctly contemporary. Distinguishing details include double serifs on lowercase letters such as 'b', 'd', 'h' and 'k', crescent-shaped round forms and a characteristically adorned lowercase 'l'. The family spans 108 styles across variable weight, width and contrast axes, with small caps, alternates and a sculptural headline sub-family, Le Grand, designed for high-contrast display use.
foundry_blurb: Newglyph describe this as the culmination of Ian Party's lifelong dialogue with the Romain du Roi — not a revival but a reimagining that unites geometric rationality with elegance, and science with craftsmanship. The collection pairs Romain du Roi, a text family crafted for rhythm and readability, with Le Grand, a theatrical headline companion built for contrast and typographic presence. Together they carry centuries of typographic legacy forward with contemporary precision and poetic flair.

name: Agatha
classification_notes: Agatha draws explicitly from 19th-century Didot and neoclassical traditions, with high contrast between thick and thin strokes and a romantic, art nouveau character typical of Didone serifs.
summary: A Didot-rooted serif by Ian Party, developed over two decades from a 19th-century typeface discovered in a Lausanne bookshop, now released as a 180-style variable family.
description: Ian Party first encountered the source typeface in 2003, inside a copy of La Comédie du jour sous la République Athénienne illustrated by Caran D'Ache. The design evolved through several iterations for editorial clients before culminating in a 2023 variable release spanning three axes: weight, width and contrast. Its romantic character shows in the curved serifs, the floral g, and the sinuous strokes of the A, V and W, situating it between high Didot formalism and early art nouveau.
foundry_blurb: Newglyph describe Agatha as a typeface with DNA tracing back to Amazonia, blending neoclassicism and 19th-century Didot with a modern, romantic sensibility. The 2023 release brings the family to 180 static styles and two variable fonts with weight, width and contrast axes, culminating Ian Party's work that began in 2003.

name: SwissPosters
classification_notes: Described explicitly as a condensed version of a Swiss modernist font, placing it firmly in the grotesque sans-serif tradition associated with Swiss International Style typography.
summary: A condensed Swiss modernist grotesque by Ian Party, designed as a tribute to contemporary Swiss poster culture and offered as a large variable family of 180 styles.
description: Inspired by the curated Swiss graphic design archive of Instagram account @SwissPosters, this family was developed in collaboration with Dennis Moya Rafafimandimby. It delivers a condensed grotesque in the Swiss International Style across 180 static styles plus a variable font, with axes controlling width, weight, x-height and italic. Roman and Tall subfamilies each have matching italics, giving the family considerable range for headline and poster work.
foundry_blurb: Newglyph describe SwissPosters as a forthcoming typeface born from a collaboration with Dennis Moya Rafafimandimby, whose Instagram account documents a curated collection of Swiss graphic design. The family captures the essence of that tradition by offering a condensed Swiss modernist font as a tribute to the dynamic world of contemporary graphic design.

name: SwissNow
classification_notes: Explicitly modelled on Swiss grotesque typefaces in the modernist tradition, placing it squarely in the grotesque subcategory of sans-serif. The design draws on the formal conventions of mid-century Swiss type such as Helvetica and Univers.
summary: A Swiss grotesque in 20 styles by Ian Party, designed as an affordable homage to mid-century modernist type tradition.
description: Part of Newglyph's SwissKnife collection, this family takes the formal conventions of Swiss grotesque typefaces as its starting point, offering a variable font alongside 20 discrete static styles. At 433 glyphs per style, it covers a broad sweep of Latin-based languages and includes standard punctuation, mathematical symbols and ligatures. Positioned as a quality-accessible entry point for designers who want the neutrality of the Swiss grotesque without a high licensing cost.
foundry_blurb: Newglyph describe SwissNow as the debut release in their SwissKnife collection and a homage to the rich legacy of modernist Swiss design. They position it as a fusion of tradition and innovation that brings the spirit of Swiss modernism into contemporary design, forming part of a collection built on accessibility without compromising quality, offering high-quality fonts at an affordable price point.

name: Angela
classification_notes: The foundry explicitly describes Angela as built on Neo-Grotesque principles, placing it in the tradition of Helvetica and Univers with its emphasis on geometric neutrality and clean forms.
summary: A neo-grotesque sans by Ian Party from Newglyph, offered in 27 styles with a variable font axis spanning weight and italic.
description: Designed by Ian Party and published by Newglyph, Angela applies neo-grotesque principles with an emphasis on simplicity and even spacing across 391 glyphs per style. The family ships as a variable font with weight and italic axes alongside 288 individual static styles in OTF, TTF and WOFF2 formats. Latin extended language support covers a broad range of African, European and Pacific Island languages.
foundry_blurb: Newglyph describe Angela as a typeface built on Neo-Grotesque principles, presented as a study in modernity, simplicity and elegance. It is designed by Ian Party and offered in 27 styles with 391 glyphs each, accompanied by a variable font.

name: NeoGeo
classification_notes: NeoGeo is described as having minimal contrast and even widths reflecting a geometric archetype, placing it squarely in the geometric sans-serif tradition with a Swiss sensibility.
summary: A geometric sans by Ian Party with minimal stroke contrast and even widths, offered in 20 styles with a variable font axis.
description: Designed by Ian Party and released by Newglyph, NeoGeo takes the even widths and low contrast of Swiss geometric sans-serifs and applies what the foundry calls a twist to ensure flexibility across design contexts. The family ships in 20 styles alongside a variable font in TTF, OTF and WOFF2 formats, with 392 glyphs per style covering a broad sweep of Latin-based languages. Pricing starts at CHF 200 for a single style.
foundry_blurb: Newglyph describe NeoGeo as a geometric typeface whose minimal contrast and even widths reflect its geometric archetype with a twist, making it adaptable to diverse design scenarios. It is designed by Ian Party and available in 20 styles plus a variable font.

name: Sunlight
classification_notes: Sunlight is described as a Didot typeface with high contrast and large proportions, placing it firmly in the modern serif (Didone) tradition, distinguished by extreme thick-to-thin stroke contrast and vertical stress.
summary: A high-contrast Didone with large-than-life proportions from Ian Party at Newglyph, available in 72 styles including variable axes for weight, width and contrast.
description: Drawing on the Didot tradition while referencing Caslon, Sunlight pairs extreme stroke contrast with generous proportions across 72 static styles and two variable fonts. The family spans Roman, Italic, Contrast and Contrast Italic subfamilies, with each style containing 455 glyphs and coverage for a broad range of Latin-script languages. The variable axes for weight, width and contrast give designers fine-grained control for display and editorial applications.
foundry_blurb: Newglyph describe Sunlight as a Didot typeface that pays homage to Caslon, with modern contrast and larger-than-life proportions intended as a forward-looking voice in typography. It comprises 72 styles with 455 glyphs each, two variable fonts, and is designed by Ian Party.

name: SwissDin
classification_notes: SwissDin is described as architectural and constructed, drawing on DIN-style geometric rationalism and Swiss design principles of form, structure and function — placing it firmly in the geometric sans category.
summary: A geometric, DIN-inspired sans by Ian Party that channels Swiss design's preoccupation with form, structure and function into 20 styles and a variable font.
description: Designed by Ian Party at Newglyph, SwissDin applies an architectural, constructed logic to the Latin alphabet, drawing on the Swiss design tradition's rigour rather than any specific historical DIN specimen. The family spans 20 styles with 431 glyphs per style and is also available as a variable font with weight and italic axes. Language support covers a broad range of Latin-script languages from Afrikaans to Zulu.
foundry_blurb: Newglyph describe SwissDin as an architectural font in a constructed style, presenting it as a reflection of Swiss design's affinity for form, structure and function. It is offered in 20 styles with 431 glyphs each, plus a variable font.

name: SwissTime
classification_notes: SwissTime draws directly from the Times lineage, a quintessential transitional serif characterised by moderate contrast, bracketed serifs and upright roman posture. The reference to Times' precision and elegance places it firmly within that category.
summary: A Times-derived transitional serif by Ian Party, offered in 12 styles with variable font support across weight and italic axes.
description: Drawing directly from the Times tradition, SwissTime pairs the familiar structure of moderate stroke contrast and bracketed serifs with a broad Latin Extended glyph set of 397 characters per style. The family ships as 12 static styles alongside two variable fonts covering weight and italic, making it adaptable for both print and screen editorial contexts. Language coverage spans a wide range of Latin-based scripts from Afrikaans to Zulu.
foundry_blurb: Newglyph describe SwissTime as a typeface inspired by the timeless aesthetics of Times, framing it as an emblem of precision and elegance. The family includes 12 styles, 397 glyphs per style, and two variable fonts, available from CHF 100 as a single or custom style licence.

name: SunKing
classification_notes: SunKing is a sans serif built on the structural principles of the Romain du Roi, a historically constructed Roman type. Its grid-based construction and precision place it in the rational or constructed sans tradition, distinct from humanist or grotesque approaches.
summary: A variable sans serif by Ian Party built on the structural logic of the Romain du Roi, Louis XIV's commissioned typeface of 1692.
description: Designed by Ian Party and published by Newglyph, SunKing translates the grid-based precision of the Romain du Roi into a contemporary sans serif with a variable weight axis spanning nine static styles. The design is not a revival but a reinterpretation, drawing on surviving specimens to inform proportions and construction without reproducing serifs or period details. With 426 glyphs per style and broad Latin Extended language support, it suits editorial and branding work that calls for historic gravitas in a modern form.
foundry_blurb: Newglyph describe SunKing as a contemporary sans serif inspired by the Romain du Roi, the typeface commissioned by Louis XIV in 1692 for the Imprimerie Royale in Paris. Ian Party developed the design on a variable font grid system to ensure consistency and precision across the character set, capturing the spirit of its historic predecessor without attempting a straight revival. The family includes nine styles, 426 glyphs per style and a single variable font, available from CHF 100.

name: Baikal
classification_notes: Explicitly described as a Grotesque typeface, built on a variable font grid system with a clean, minimal aesthetic consistent with the grotesque tradition.
summary: A variable grotesque from Ian Party at Newglyph offering 144 styles across weight and width axes, designed around simplicity and a clean modern aesthetic.
description: Built on a variable font grid system, Baikal spans 144 static styles with a single variable font covering weight, width and italic axes. Each style carries 434 glyphs with broad Latin Extended language support. Used in production by clients including Havas, Bob Industries and the National Theatre of Scotland, it sits comfortably in branding, editorial and screen contexts.
foundry_blurb: Newglyph describe Baikal as a grotesque typeface designed with precision on a variable font grid system, capturing a clean, modern aesthetic. They highlight its 144 styles, 434 glyphs per style and variable font functionality as tools for creative flexibility, and cite it as one of their bestsellers among professional designers.

name: Amazonia
classification_notes: Amazonia is explicitly modelled on the Didot archetype, featuring extreme contrast between thick and thin strokes characteristic of French neoclassical didone serifs.
summary: A high-contrast didone with extreme thick-thin stroke variation, issued in 180 styles with variable font axes for weight, width and contrast.
description: Designed by Ian Party and named after the legendary Amazons of Greek mythology, this typeface draws on the French neoclassical Didot tradition as its structural model. The family spans 180 static styles across Roman, Italic, Contrast and Contrast Italic subfamilies, accompanied by two variable font files offering continuous control over weight, width and contrast. Each style contains 547 glyphs with broad Latin Extended coverage across more than 70 languages.
foundry_blurb: Newglyph describe Amazonia as a tribute to strength and refinement, channelling the fearless spirit of the Amazons through the powerful contrast of the Didot archetype. The variable font format grants control over weight, width and other typographic attributes from a single file, while the full package encompasses 180 styles in OTF, TTF and WOFF2 formats for print and web use.

name: Atacama
classification_notes: The foundry explicitly classifies Atacama under the Garalde category, citing bracketed serifs, modulated stroke contrast and humanist curves characteristic of old-style typefaces in the Aldine tradition.
summary: A Garalde serif by Ian Party with bracketed serifs, curved droplets and a variable axis spanning weight, width and contrast across 180 styles.
description: Designed by Ian Party, Atacama takes the Garalde model as its framework, working with modulated thick-to-thin strokes, bracketed serifs and wide rounded apertures that aid legibility at text sizes. The family ships as two variable fonts covering Roman and Italic, with weight, width and contrast axes, generating 180 discrete static styles as a byproduct. With 399 glyphs per style and broad Latin language support, it targets print, web and screen applications equally.
foundry_blurb: Newglyph describe Atacama as a tribute to the Atacama Desert, with horizontal crossbars, peaks and humanist curves echoing the landscape's arid beauty. Belonging to the Garalde classification, it moves between thick and thin strokes with bracketed serifs and elegantly curved droplets. The package includes a single variable font generating 180 styles, available in OTF, TTF and WOFF2, and is positioned as a versatile Garalde archetype for print, web and digital design.

name: Armada
classification_notes: Explicitly described as incorporating Neo-Grotesque principles, with the clean, neutral construction typical of that genre. The variable weight axis and minimal glyph set reinforce a modernist, functional approach.
summary: A neo-grotesque sans by Ian Party, offered as a variable font with eight static styles and broad Latin language coverage.
description: Designed by Ian Party and published through Newglyph, Armada follows neo-grotesque conventions with a clean, upright construction suited to branding and typographic applications. The family ships as a variable font spanning a weight axis, alongside 382-glyph static styles in OTF, TTF and WOFF2 formats. Language support is extensive across Latin-based writing systems, covering dozens of African, European and Southeast Asian languages.
foundry_blurb: Newglyph describe Armada as a typeface built on Neo-Grotesque principles, positioning it as an expression of modernity, simplicity and elegance. It is available in 8 styles with 382 glyphs per style and a single variable font, starting from CHF 50.

name: Alaska
classification_notes: Built from circles, triangles and rectangles in the tradition of Bauhaus-era geometric sans serifs; the variable axes cover weight, width, italic and contrast, keeping stroke contrast low and forms highly constructed throughout.
summary: A geometric variable sans by Ian Party spanning 240 styles across weight, width, italic and contrast axes, drawing from the Bauhaus tradition of elemental forms.
description: Built from circles, triangles and rectangles, the typeface follows the geometric archetype established in the 1920s and 1930s, keeping stroke contrast minimal and widths even across the family. A single variable font covers four axes simultaneously, with 240 static styles also available in OTF, TTF and WOFF2. Latin coverage is broad, spanning over 80 languages including African and European scripts.
foundry_blurb: Newglyph describe Alaska as a geometric variable typeface named after the Aleut word Alaxsxaq meaning mainland. Drawing on the elemental typography movement catalysed by the Bauhaus, it embraces simple geometric forms and offers a variable font with 240 styles, providing control over weight, width, italic and contrast axes for contemporary design applications.

name: Oddity
classification_notes: The foundry explicitly describes Oddity as incorporating neo-grotesque principles, and the glyph set and variable axis structure are consistent with a modern neo-grotesque sans-serif.
summary: A variable neo-grotesque by Ian Party offering 132 styles across weight and width axes with broad Latin language coverage.
description: Designed by Ian Party and released through Newglyph, Oddity draws on neo-grotesque conventions to produce a clean, neutral sans-serif in 132 static styles plus a variable font spanning weight, width and italic axes. The character set covers 427 glyphs per style, including extended Latin accented characters, ligatures, stylistic alternates for several letters including R, e, r and y, and a full range of mathematical symbols. Language support stretches across a large number of Latin-script languages from Afrikaans and Catalan to Swahili and Zulu.
foundry_blurb: Newglyph describe Oddity as a typeface that incorporates neo-grotesque principles, positioning it as an expression of modernity, simplicity and elegance. It ships as 132 single styles plus one variable font, with 427 glyphs per style and pricing from CHF 100.

name: Crystal
classification_notes: Described explicitly as a modern sans-serif or grotesque typeface, drawing on the Akzidenz-Grotesk tradition with a monoline design and unadorned, sleek character forms.
summary: A monoline grotesque by Ian Party that revisits the Akzidenz tradition with subtle contemporary refinements across 144 styles and a variable font axis.
description: Drawing on the Akzidenz-Grotesk lineage, Crystal offers a clean, unadorned monoline construction with understated distinctions from its historical reference. The family spans 144 styles with both Roman and Italic cuts, supported by a variable font covering width, weight and italic axes. Coverage extends to a wide range of Latin-script languages across African, European and South-East Asian languages.
foundry_blurb: Newglyph describe Crystal as a unique take on 'Akzidenz', positioning it as a modern sans-serif or grotesque with subtle yet distinctive differences from its reference. Designed by Ian Party, it is characterised as unadorned and sleek with a monoline design, embodying contemporary elegance across 144 styles and 434 glyphs per style.

name: Aloha
classification_notes: Aloha is explicitly a monospaced typeface, distinguished by its deliberate mixing of serif and sans-serif letterforms within the same family, making it a hybrid mono rather than a purely grotesque or slab mono.
summary: A monospaced typeface by Ian Party that deliberately mixes serif and sans-serif letterforms within the same character set, spanning five weights with a variable axis.
description: Selective serifs appear on certain lowercase letters such as f, g, i, j, l and t, and on uppercase characters including C, G, I, J and S, while the remaining glyphs follow a clean sans-serif structure. The contrast between the two conventions is treated as a feature rather than an inconsistency, giving the typeface a distinctive rhythm within its fixed-width grid. Available in OTF, TTF and WOFF2 formats, including a variable font file and 367 individual static styles.
foundry_blurb: Newglyph describe Aloha as a monospace font inspired by the Hawaiian word's qualities of love, peace and compassion, combining the elegance of serif and sans-serif letters to bring two opposites into harmonious coexistence. Long horizontal brackets appear on select lowercase letters and sharp serifs on certain uppercase characters, while the rest follow classic sans-serif tradition, available across five styles from thin to black.

name: Azteca
classification_notes: Azteca blends didone high-contrast structure with monospaced geometry, featuring extra-long serifs and dramatic thick-to-thin stroke contrast that push it firmly into the display category rather than text use.
summary: A didone-monospaced hybrid by Ian Party, distinguished by extra-long serifs and high stroke contrast across three weights with a variable contrast axis.
description: Ian Party combined the rigid, vertical structure of 18th-century didone type with the fixed-width logic of monospaced fonts, producing a face with distinctive long serifs and sharp contrast between thick and thin strokes. Quirks include perfectly round terminals on lowercase letters and jaguar fang-like stems on the uppercase W. Three named styles — Headline, Kingsize and Text — are accompanied by a variable font with a customisable contrast axis running from 1 to 100.
foundry_blurb: Newglyph describe Azteca as a fusion of didone and monospaced typography, created by Ian Party through a design process that involved overcoming structural challenges to produce surprising visual solutions. The font's extra-long serifs and stroke contrast give it dramatic character, while carefully crafted exceptions to its rigid structure maintain visual rhythm and readability across three weights and a variable version.

name: Alpaga
classification_notes: Alpaga is explicitly designed as a monospace typeface, with uniform character widths across all styles, available as a variable font with axes for weight, width and italic.
summary: A variable monospace typeface by Ian Party spanning 96 styles from Extra Condensed Hairline to Ultra Expanded Extra Black.
description: Designed by Ian Party and published by Newglyph, Alpaga covers an unusually wide stylistic range for a monospace family, with weight, width and italic axes available through a single variable font file. Each character maintains a consistent width across all styles, preserving the structured rhythm typical of monospaced type. The package ships in TTF, OTF and WOFF2 formats and covers an extensive range of Latin-script languages.
foundry_blurb: Newglyph describe Alpaga as a monospace variable typeface inspired by the resilience and adaptability of the alpaca, offering 96 styles spanning from Extra Condensed Hairline to Ultra Expanded Extra Black. The variable font gives designers control over weight, width and italic axes, while the monospace construction ensures uniform character widths throughout.

name: Azaka
classification_notes: Described as an asymmetrical sans-serif hybrid, the design's organic irregularities and cultural references align it with humanist sans conventions rather than geometric or grotesque traditions.
summary: A variable sans-serif by Ian Party with deliberate asymmetry, named after the Haitian agricultural deity Azaka.
description: The typeface's asymmetrical construction is said to mirror the organic variation of the earth and crops, drawing on the cultural heritage of Haiti and its indigenous Taino language. Available in 11 styles with a variable font axis covering the full weight range, it supports an extensive Latin character set across over 80 languages. The 389 glyphs per style include a comprehensive set of accented characters, ligatures and mathematical symbols.
foundry_blurb: Newglyph describe Azaka as a versatile and asymmetrical sans-serif hybrid, named after Azaka, the Haitian patron deity of agriculture, also known as Papa Zaka, Mazaka and Kouzen. The name is thought to derive from the Taino language, possibly linked to words for corn or maize. The variable font offers 11 styles and is positioned as a typographic companion that embraces the diversity and beauty of the harvest, with limitless adaptability across design applications.

name: Stardust
classification_notes: The foundry explicitly positions Stardust within neo-grotesque principles, emphasising modernity and simplicity — hallmarks of the neo-grotesque genre derived from mid-century Swiss typography.
summary: A neo-grotesque sans by Ian Party from Newglyph, offered in 132 styles with a variable font axis covering weight, width and italic.
description: Designed by Ian Party and released through Newglyph, Stardust draws on neo-grotesque principles to produce a clean, modern sans-serif with 431 glyphs per style and broad Latin language coverage spanning over 80 languages. The family ships as 132 static styles alongside a single variable font covering weight, width and italic axes. The glyph set includes ligatures, stylistic alternates and an extensive range of accented characters suited to European language publishing.
foundry_blurb: Newglyph describe Stardust as a typeface built on neo-grotesque principles, framing it as an expression of modernity, simplicity and elegance. It is available in 132 styles with 431 glyphs per style, a variable font, and a starting price of CHF 100.

name: Africa
classification_notes: Extreme stroke contrast inspired by Caslon and calligraphic lettering, with sharp peaked terminals and hook-like serifs; designed expressly for large display use rather than text setting.
summary: A three-style display collection by Ian Party drawing on Caslon and calligraphic lettering to produce extreme stroke contrast and sharp, distinctive terminals.
description: Three styles — Drylands, Rainfall and Cliff — each interpret high contrast and organic calligraphic structure differently, with Africa Cliff pushing contrast to its furthest point through mega-bold strokes paired against hairline lines. Details such as fish-hook serifs on C and G, sharp peaked terminals on E, F and Z, and a blooming ampersand give the collection a distinctive visual character suited to editorial display and identity work. The 359-glyph set covers an extensive range of Latin-based languages including many African languages.
foundry_blurb: Newglyph describe Africa as a typographic ode to the Earth's second-largest continent, comprising three distinct styles — Africa Drylands, Africa Rainfall and Africa Cliff. Africa Cliff is presented as the most opulent of the three, with contrast turned to its highest point, sharp-peaked terminals, fish-hook serifs and a blooming ampersand inspired by Caslon and calligraphic lettering.
